name: On a Jazz Tune Loop
name_original: Sur un air de Jazz — boucle autour de Marciac
description: This moderate 43.0-mile (69.2 km) road cycling loop around Marciac offers scenic views of the Gers countryside and historic villages.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Marciac traverse the gently rolling hills and agricultural landscapes of Gascony. The region is characterized by quiet country roads that wind through vineyards, sunflower fields, and small villages. Elevations are moderate, offering varied terrain suitable for different fitness levels without significant mountain passes. Best road cycling routes around Marciac The most popular road cycling route is On a Jazz Tune Loop, a 42.9 miles (69.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 12 minutes to complete, offering a comprehensive exploration of the local countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Roadbike loop from Marciac, a moderate 27.5 miles (44.2 km) path. This route features a mix of open fields and tree-lined sections, with gradual ascents and descents. Local road cyclists also love the Mirande – Bastide of Bassoues loop from Marciac, a 37.5 miles (60.3 km) trail leading through historical bastide towns and rural scenery, often completed in about 2 hours 48 minutes. Road cycling around Marciac is defined by rolling hills, agricultural landscapes, and quiet country roads. The network offers options for easy road bike routes near Marciac as well as more advanced road cycling routes. The routes in Marciac are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from 8 reviews. More than 230 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Marciac's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Marciac?
A: There are over 35 road cycling routes around Marciac. These include a variety of options, with 8 easy routes, 22 moderate routes, and 5 more challenging routes, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Marciac?
A: The most pleasant times for road cycling in Marciac are typically spring (April to June) and early autumn (September to October). During these months, the weather is generally mild, and the landscapes are vibrant with blooming fields or autumn colors, making for comfortable rides through the Gascon countryside.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Marciac offers several easy road cycling routes. A good option for beginners or families is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/28778759" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fountain – Marciac loop from Marciac&lt;/a&gt;, which is approximately 26.4 miles (42.5 km) long with moderate elevation gain, offering a gentle introduction to the region's quiet country roads.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Marciac?
A: Road cycling routes around Marciac feature gently rolling hills and agricultural landscapes. You'll encounter quiet country roads winding through vineyards, sunflower fields, and small villages. Elevations are moderate, providing varied terrain without significant mountain passes, making it enjoyable for a range of fitness levels.

Q: Are there any advanced or challenging road cycling routes in the Marciac area?
A: While Marciac is known for its rolling hills, there are routes that offer more challenge. The region features 5 routes classified as difficult. These routes typically involve longer distances and more significant elevation changes, providing a good workout for experienced cyclists looking to push their limits.

Q: What are some scenic road cycling routes with good viewpoints around Marciac?
A: Many routes offer scenic views of the Gascon countryside. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1275437039/on-a-jazz-tune-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;On a Jazz Tune Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for example, provides a comprehensive exploration of the local scenery, passing through diverse agricultural landscapes and offering picturesque vistas of the rolling hills and charming villages.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes starting and ending in Marciac?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Marciac are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6981525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Marciac&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate option, offering a mix of open fields and tree-lined sections, perfect for a round trip.

Q: What attractions or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: As you cycle, you can explore several historical and natural attractions. Notable sights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6047807" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Our Lady of the Assumption of Marciac&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2700350" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bastide de Bassoues&lt;/a&gt;, and the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5860144" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabournieu Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes pass through charming villages and past historical churches, offering cultural points of interest.

Q: Are there less crowded road cycling routes for a more peaceful experience?
A: The Marciac region is generally known for its quiet country roads, making most routes relatively uncrowded. To find particularly peaceful rides, consider exploring some of the lesser-known loops that venture deeper into the agricultural areas, away from main village thoroughfares. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20526882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Marciac – Marciac loop from Marciac&lt;/a&gt; offers a serene experience through the local countryside.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Marciac?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from 8 reviews. Cyclists often praise the region's quiet country roads, the beautiful agricultural landscapes, and the varied terrain that offers both gentle rides and more challenging sections. The charm of the small villages and the overall peaceful atmosphere are frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there options for refreshments or cafes along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near small villages where you can find local cafes, bakeries, or small shops to refuel. Marciac itself offers several options, and routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/26218588" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirande – Bastide of Bassoues loop from Marciac&lt;/a&gt; will take you through historical bastide towns where you can find places to stop for a break and enjoy local specialties.

Q: Is parking available for road cyclists in Marciac?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in Marciac, particularly in and around the town center. There are public parking areas where you can leave your vehicle before starting your ride. It's advisable to check local signage for any time limits or specific regulations.
tips:
Marciac is famous for its annual jazz festival, one of the 3 biggest Jazz festivals in the world with Breacon in Wales and New Orleans in the United States.

The arcades and the main square of the city host many cafes and restaurants.
The only gate that gave access to the citadel of Villecomtal, today the only trace of its existence.

Founded in 1284, the creation of the royal road from Auch to Tarbes in the 18th century boosted the local economy.

Today, the old town offers you interesting architecture as well as cafes, restaurants and bakeries.

Near the lake is a shaded picnic area. However, swimming is not supervised there.

You will also find a view of the lake from the nearby road.

faqs.question: Where can I park when cycling the On a Jazz Tune loop around Marciac?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the town of Marciac itself, which serves as a convenient starting point for this loop. Look for designated parking areas within the town.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a road cycling trail, which means it's generally not suitable for dogs to run alongside due to traffic and the pace of cycling. If you plan to bring a dog, it would need to be safely carried in a bike trailer or basket.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Gers region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions from spring through early autumn. Cycling during the Jazz in Marciac festival in late July/early August can be a unique experience, though the area will be busier. Spring and early autumn provide comfortable temperatures and beautiful scenery.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It uses public roads and is freely accessible.

faqs.question: What is the terrain and difficulty level of the route?
faqs.answer: This is classified as a moderate road cycling route. It covers approximately 69.2 kilometers (43 miles) with an elevation gain of about 690 meters (2,264 feet). The surfaces are mostly well-paved, but a good fitness level is recommended due to the distance and elevation changes. You'll experience green hills and valleys typical of the Gers countryside.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or points of interest can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a blend of natural beauty and historical charm. You'll pass by the Church of Our Lady of the Assumption of Marciac, the Citadel Gate of Villecomtal-sur-Arros, and the serene Lac de Monpardiac. The town of Miélan is also along the loop. Keep an eye out for historic bastides and castles, and on clear days, you might even catch glimpses of the distant Pyrenees.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself winds through peaceful countryside, the towns and villages along the way, particularly Marciac, offer opportunities for refreshments. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, but you can plan stops in the larger settlements for cafes or restaurants.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other paths such as PR7 :Circuit des coteaux, Passage du Chemin Bleu, and Chemin d'Autefage, though it primarily follows its own distinct loop.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, distance of 69.2 km, and nearly 700 meters of elevation gain, this route is best suited for cyclists with a good level of fitness and some experience with road cycling. Beginners might find the distance and climbs challenging.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling this loop?
faqs.answer: You can expect to immerse yourself in the natural landscapes of the Gers countryside. The region is characterized by rolling green hills, offering picturesque views over the Arros Valley. The route is enhanced by charming historic bastides, quaint villages, and impressive castles, providing a peaceful discovery of the 'valleys of oxygen'.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the On a Jazz Tune loop?
faqs.answer: The estimated time to complete this 69.2-kilometer loop is about 3 hours and 12 minutes, assuming a steady cycling pace. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and weather conditions.

waypoints.name: Church of Our Lady of the Assumption of Marciac
